title: Cost Behavior and Classification
description: Understand how costs behave and are classified for management decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Understanding cost behavior is FOUNDATIONAL. Every planning, budgeting, and break-even analysis depends on knowing which costs change with activity and which don't. CMAs must classify costs correctly to provide useful decision information.

sections.title: Cost Behavior Patterns
sections.content:
**Variable Costs:**
• Change in TOTAL as activity changes
• Constant PER UNIT
• Examples: Direct materials, direct labor, sales commissions

**Fixed Costs:**
• Constant in TOTAL within relevant range
• Change PER UNIT as activity changes
• Examples: Rent, salaries, depreciation (straight-line)

**Mixed (Semi-variable) Costs:**
• Contain both fixed and variable components
• Examples: Utilities, phone, maintenance

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fixed vs. Variable
sections.content:
**"Total vs. Per Unit - They're Opposites!"**

**Variable:**
• Total varies (up and down)
• Per unit stays constant (fixed rate)

**Fixed:**
• Total stays fixed (constant)
• Per unit varies (spreads over more units)

**Remember: What's fixed/variable in TOTAL is opposite PER UNIT!**

sections.title: Relevant Range
sections.content:
**Definition:**
The range of activity over which cost behavior assumptions are valid.

**Outside the relevant range:**
• Fixed costs may change (need more space, equipment)
• Variable cost per unit may change (quantity discounts, overtime)

**Example:**
• Rent is fixed at $10,000/month for 0-10,000 sq ft
• At 10,001 sq ft, need additional space → rent jumps to $18,000

**Always specify the relevant range for cost estimates!**

sections.title: High-Low Method
sections.content:
**Simple technique to separate mixed costs:**

**Step 1: Variable Cost per Unit**
(High Cost - Low Cost) / (High Activity - Low Activity)

**Step 2: Fixed Cost**
Total Cost - (Variable Rate × Activity Level)

**Example:**
High: 10,000 units, $45,000 cost
Low: 6,000 units, $33,000 cost

Variable: ($45,000 - $33,000) / (10,000 - 6,000) = $3/unit
Fixed: $45,000 - ($3 × 10,000) = $15,000

**Cost function:** Y = $15,000 + $3X

sections.title: Product vs. Period Costs
sections.content:
**Product Costs (Inventoriable):**
• Attached to inventory until sold
• DM, DL, Manufacturing Overhead
• Become COGS when sold

**Period Costs:**
• Expensed in period incurred
• Selling expenses
• Administrative expenses
• Not attached to products

**Key difference:** Product costs sit on balance sheet (inventory) until sale; period costs hit income statement immediately.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Step Costs
sections.content:
**Step costs are fixed within ranges, then jump:**

• Supervisor salaries (1 per 10 workers)
• Equipment rental (per machine)
• Storage space

**Treatment on exam:**
• Often treated as fixed within relevant range
• May need to identify the "step" when crossing ranges
• Watch for questions asking about cost at specific activity levels

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Variable costs change in total but are constant per unit
Fixed costs are constant in total but change per unit
Relevant range defines where cost behavior holds true
High-Low Method: (High - Low Cost) / (High - Low Activity) = Variable rate
Product costs attach to inventory; Period costs expense immediately
Different classifications serve different purposes
Step costs are fixed within ranges, then jump

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding cost behavior within the relevant range?
options: Total variable costs remain constant as production increases.
Per-unit fixed costs remain constant as production increases.
Total fixed costs increase proportionally with production.
Per-unit variable costs remain constant as production increases.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Per-unit variable costs are constant within the relevant range because each unit incurs the same cost for materials, labor, etc. Total variable costs increase with production. Total fixed costs remain constant, while per-unit fixed costs decrease as production increases.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what happens on a PER UNIT basis for each type of cost.

reveal.question: Define 'relevant range' and explain its importance in cost accounting.
reveal.answer: The relevant range is the range of activity over which cost behavior assumptions (e.g., fixed costs remaining constant in total) are valid. It's important because cost predictions outside this range may be inaccurate, leading to poor decision-making.

knowledgeCheck.question: Using the high-low method, what is the estimated fixed cost component if the highest activity level is 12,000 units with a total cost of $52,000, and the lowest activity level is 7,000 units with a total cost of $34,500?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the variable cost per unit: ($52,000 - $34,500) / (12,000 - 7,000) = $3.50 per unit.  Then, calculate the fixed cost using either the high or low activity level. Using the high activity level: $52,000 - ($3.50 * 12,000) = $10,000. Using the low activity level: $34,500 - ($3.50 * 7,000) = $10,000.  Therefore, the fixed cost is $17,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the two steps of the high-low method: variable cost per unit, then fixed cost.

title: Job Order Costing
description: Calculate product costs using job order costing systems

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Job order costing tracks costs to specific jobs, products, or batches. CMAs use it for custom manufacturing, construction, and professional services. Understanding job costing is essential for accurate product costing and pricing.

sections.title: When to Use Job Order Costing
sections.content:
**Appropriate when:**
• Products are distinct/customized
• Each job is identifiable
• Costs differ significantly between jobs

**Industries:**
• Custom furniture manufacturing
• Construction
• Printing shops
• Consulting/professional services
• Repair shops
• Movie production

sections.title: Job Cost Sheet
sections.content:
**The key document that accumulates costs for each job:**

```
Job Order Cost Sheet
Job No: 101
Customer: ABC Corp
Date Started: 1/5    Date Completed: 1/20

Direct Materials:
  Requisition #201    $2,000
  Requisition #205      $800
  Total DM           $2,800

Direct Labor:
  40 hrs @ $25/hr    $1,000
  20 hrs @ $30/hr      $600
  Total DL           $1,600

Manufacturing Overhead Applied:
  60 hrs × $15/DLH     $900

Total Job Cost:       $5,300
```

sections.title: Overhead Application
sections.content:
**Predetermined Overhead Rate (POHR):**

POHR = Estimated OH / Estimated Activity Base

**Common allocation bases:**
• Direct labor hours
• Direct labor dollars
• Machine hours
• Direct materials cost

**Overhead Applied:**
Actual Activity × POHR

**Example:**
POHR = $300,000 / 20,000 DLH = $15/DLH
Job 101 used 60 DLH
OH Applied = 60 × $15 = $900

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: OH Flow
sections.content:
**"APAR"** - What happens to overhead:

**A**ctual OH → Debit OH Control (incurred)
**P**redetermined rate calculated
**A**pplied OH → Credit OH Control, Debit WIP
**R**econcile at year-end (close difference)

**Actual ≠ Applied (almost always!)**

sections.title: Over/Underapplied Overhead
sections.content:
**At year-end:**
Actual OH incurred vs. OH Applied

**Underapplied (Debit balance):**
Actual OH > Applied OH
"We didn't charge enough to products"

**Overapplied (Credit balance):**
Applied OH > Actual OH
"We charged too much to products"

**Disposition:**
• If immaterial: Close to COGS
• If material: Allocate to WIP, FG, and COGS based on overhead in each

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Actual vs. Applied
sections.content:
**Never use actual OH for job costing during the period!**

**Why?**
• Actual OH not known until period ends
• Need cost information for pricing decisions NOW
• Some OH is seasonal (e.g., heating costs)

**Use POHR and applied OH for:**
• Pricing decisions
• Job profitability analysis
• Inventory valuation

**Reconcile to actual only at period end!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Job order costing traces costs to specific jobs/batches
Job cost sheet accumulates DM + DL + Applied OH
POHR = Estimated OH / Estimated Activity
Applied OH = Actual Activity × POHR
Underapplied: Actual > Applied (charged too little)
Overapplied: Applied > Actual (charged too much)
Immaterial over/under → close to COGS

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following industries would MOST likely use job order costing?
options: A manufacturer of identical plastic storage containers.
A large-scale producer of breakfast cereal.
A construction company building custom homes.
An oil refinery processing crude oil.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Construction companies building custom homes produce distinct, customized products. Job order costing is ideal for tracking costs associated with each individual home built. The other options involve mass production of homogeneous goods, where process costing would be more appropriate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the nature of the product or service provided by each industry. Does it involve customized work?

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the Predetermined Overhead Rate (POHR)?
reveal.answer: Predetermined Overhead Rate (POHR) = Estimated Total Overhead Costs / Estimated Total Activity Base

knowledgeCheck.question: A company estimates its overhead costs to be $500,000 and expects to use 25,000 direct labor hours. During the year, the company actually incurred $480,000 in overhead costs and used 24,000 direct labor hours. How much overhead should be applied to production during the year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the POHR: $500,000 / 25,000 DLH = $20/DLH. Then, calculate the overhead applied: 24,000 DLH * $20/DLH = $480,000. Note that the actual overhead costs are irrelevant when determining the *applied* overhead.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the POHR *before* determining the applied overhead. Use the *actual* activity level to apply overhead.

title: Process Costing
description: Calculate product costs using process costing and equivalent units

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Process costing averages costs over identical units in continuous production. It's used in chemicals, food processing, oil refining, and semiconductors. CMAs must calculate equivalent units to properly value inventory when units are partially complete.

sections.title: When to Use Process Costing
sections.content:
**Appropriate when:**
• Products are homogeneous (identical)
• Continuous production process
• Costs cannot be traced to individual units

**Industries:**
• Oil refining
• Chemical processing
• Food and beverage
• Pharmaceuticals
• Cement
• Paper manufacturing

sections.title: The Problem: Partially Complete Units
sections.content:
**At any point, production has:**
• Beginning WIP (started last period)
• Units started and completed
• Ending WIP (not yet finished)

**Question:** How do we calculate cost per unit when some units aren't finished?

**Solution: Equivalent Units (EU)**
Convert partial units to their equivalent in fully completed units.

**Example:**
1,000 units 40% complete = 400 equivalent units

sections.title: Equivalent Units Calculation
sections.content:
**Weighted Average Method:**
EU = Units Completed + (Ending WIP × % Complete)

**FIFO Method:**
EU = (Beg WIP × % to Complete) + Units Started & Completed + (Ending WIP × % Complete)

**Key difference:**
• Weighted Average: Blends beginning inventory with current period
• FIFO: Separates beginning inventory from current period work

**Note:** Materials often added at beginning (100%), conversion at various points.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FIFO EU
sections.content:
**"Complete, Start Fresh, End Partial"**

FIFO Equivalent Units:

1. **Complete** the beginning WIP (what's left to do)
2. **Start Fresh** - units started AND completed
3. **End Partial** - ending WIP progress

**Units Started & Completed = Units Started - Ending WIP**
OR
**= Units Completed - Beginning WIP**

sections.title: Process Costing Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Beginning WIP: 2,000 units (40% complete for conversion)
• Started: 10,000 units
• Completed: 9,000 units
• Ending WIP: 3,000 units (60% complete for conversion)
• Beginning WIP cost: $8,000
• Current period conversion costs: $72,000

**Weighted Average EU:**
9,000 + (3,000 × 60%) = 9,000 + 1,800 = 10,800 EU

**Cost per EU (WA):**
($8,000 + $72,000) / 10,800 = $7.41/EU

**FIFO EU:**
(2,000 × 60%) + (9,000 - 2,000) + (3,000 × 60%)
= 1,200 + 7,000 + 1,800 = 10,000 EU

**Cost per EU (FIFO):**
$72,000 / 10,000 = $7.20/EU

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Materials vs. Conversion
sections.content:
**Materials and conversion often have DIFFERENT % complete!**

**Common pattern:**
• Materials added at START → 100% complete for all WIP
• Conversion added evenly → varies based on stage

**Example:**
Ending WIP: 1,000 units, 100% materials, 40% conversion

EU for materials: 1,000 × 100% = 1,000
EU for conversion: 1,000 × 40% = 400

**Calculate separately, cost separately, add together!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Process costing for homogeneous products in continuous production
Equivalent Units convert partial units to completed equivalents
WA EU = Completed + (Ending WIP × %)
FIFO EU separates beginning WIP from current period work
Materials often 100% at start; Conversion varies
Calculate materials and conversion EU separately
Cost reconciliation: Total in = Total out (check your work!)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following industries would MOST likely use process costing?
options: A company producing specialized medical devices.
A large-scale producer of breakfast cereal.
A construction company building unique residential homes.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Process costing is best suited for industries with continuous production of homogeneous products. A large-scale producer of breakfast cereal fits this description because they produce a standardized product in large quantities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which industry produces identical products on a large scale.

reveal.question: What is the primary difference between the Weighted Average and FIFO methods in process costing regarding the treatment of beginning work-in-process (WIP) inventory?
reveal.answer: Weighted Average combines the costs and units of beginning WIP with the current period's costs and units, while FIFO separates the beginning WIP inventory and its associated costs from the current period's production activities.

knowledgeCheck.question: Under the weighted-average method, what is the formula for calculating equivalent units?
options: (Beg WIP × % to Complete) + Units Started & Completed + (Ending WIP × % to Complete)
Beginning WIP + Units Started - Ending WIP
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The weighted-average method calculates equivalent units by adding the number of units completed to the equivalent units in ending work-in-process inventory (Ending WIP × % to Complete). It doesn't separate out beginning WIP.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that weighted average blends everything together.

sections.title: Equivalent Units: FIFO vs. Weighted Average

rows.label: Beginning WIP
values: Considers the work needed to complete beginning WIP.
Ignores the stage of completion of beginning WIP. Treats as if started and completed this period.

rows.label: Cost Calculation
values: Keeps beginning WIP costs separate from current period costs.
Blends beginning WIP costs with current period costs.

rows.label: Complexity
values: More complex due to the need to track beginning WIP separately.
Simpler to calculate as it combines all costs and units.

title: Activity-Based Costing (ABC)
description: Apply ABC to calculate accurate product costs using cost drivers

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional costing uses one overhead rate and often subsidizes complex products. ABC assigns overhead based on what actually drives costs. CMAs use ABC to reveal true product profitability and identify cost reduction opportunities.

sections.title: The Problem with Traditional Costing
sections.content:
**Traditional (plantwide or departmental) overhead:**
• Uses volume-based allocations (DLH, machine hours)
• Assumes all products consume OH in proportion to volume

**Result:**
• High-volume simple products → Overcosted
• Low-volume complex products → Undercosted

**Why?** Complex products require more:
• Setups, inspections, engineering changes
• Special handling, smaller batches
• These costs don't correlate with volume!

sections.title: ABC Approach
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Identify activities that consume resources

**Step 2:** Assign costs to activity cost pools

**Step 3:** Identify cost drivers for each activity

**Step 4:** Calculate activity rate = Pool Cost / Total Driver Units

**Step 5:** Apply costs to products based on actual driver consumption

**Key insight:** Assign costs based on what CAUSES the cost!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cost Hierarchy
sections.content:
**"U-B-P-F"** - From specific to broad:

**U**nit-level: Each unit (machining, direct materials)
**B**atch-level: Each batch (setups, inspections)
**P**roduct-level: Each product (engineering, design)
**F**acility-level: Overall capacity (rent, plant manager)

**Only unit-level varies with production volume!**

sections.title: Product Cost Comparison
sections.content:
**Product A (high volume, simple):**
• 10,000 units, 10 setups, 100 inspections
• ABC OH: (10 × $500) + (100 × $50) = $10,000
• Per unit: $1.00

**Product B (low volume, complex):**
• 1,000 units, 30 setups, 150 inspections
• ABC OH: (30 × $500) + (150 × $50) = $22,500
• Per unit: $22.50

**Traditional (based on units):**
Products appear equally profitable

**ABC reveals:**
Product B is 22× more overhead-intensive per unit!

sections.title: ABC Implementation
sections.content:
**Benefits:**
• More accurate product costs
• Better pricing decisions
• Identifies process improvement opportunities
• Supports product mix decisions

**Challenges:**
• Time and cost to implement
• Requires activity analysis
• Cost driver selection can be subjective
• Need to maintain/update system

**Best for:**
• Diverse product lines
• Significant indirect costs
• Non-volume-related activities

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ABC Doesn't Always Differ
sections.content:
**When does ABC produce SIMILAR results to traditional?**

• All products are similar (single product company)
• Most costs are truly volume-driven
• Low overhead relative to direct costs
• No product diversity

**ABC matters most when:**
• Product diversity is high
• Overhead is significant
• Batch and product-sustaining activities are major cost drivers

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Traditional costing uses volume bases; ABC uses causal drivers
ABC cost hierarchy: Unit, Batch, Product, Facility
Only unit-level costs vary with production volume
Complex/low-volume products are often undercosted traditionally
Activity Rate = Cost Pool / Driver Quantity
ABC reveals true product profitability
Implementation requires activity analysis and driver selection

sections.title: Traditional vs. ABC Costing: Which is Which?

knowledgeCheck.question: Under a traditional costing system that relies solely on direct labor hours, a high-volume, simple product will most likely be:
options: Undercosted, leading to potentially inflated profit margins.
Overcosted, potentially leading to reduced competitiveness.
Accurately costed, as direct labor hours are the primary cost driver.
Impossible to determine without knowing the overhead rate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Traditional costing systems, especially those using volume-based allocations like direct labor hours, tend to overcost high-volume, simple products because they allocate overhead based on volume rather than actual resource consumption. Simple products consume fewer resources than complex products, but get a larger share of OH.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how overhead costs are allocated in relation to the product's complexity and resource consumption.

reveal.question: What is the correct order of steps in Activity-Based Costing (ABC)?
reveal.answer:
1. Identify activities that consume resources.
2. Assign costs to activity cost pools.
3. Identify cost drivers for each activity.
4. Calculate activity rate = Pool Cost / Total Driver Units.
5. Apply costs to products based on actual driver consumption.

sections.title: Traditional vs. ABC: A Side-by-Side Look

rows.label: Cost Allocation Base
values: Volume-based (e.g., DLH, machine hours)
Activity-based (cost drivers specific to each activity)

rows.label: Overhead Allocation
values: Allocates overhead based on a single or few plant-wide/departmental rates
Allocates overhead based on activities and their related cost drivers

rows.label: Product Cost Accuracy
values: Less accurate, especially for diverse product lines
More accurate, especially for complex, low-volume products

rows.label: Complexity
values: Simpler to implement
More complex and costly to implement

title: Variable and Absorption Costing
description: Compare variable and absorption costing methods and their impact on income

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Absorption costing (GAAP) and variable costing (internal) produce different income figures when production ≠ sales. CMAs must understand when and why income differs, reconcile between methods, and know when each is appropriate.

sections.title: The Key Difference
sections.content:
**Fixed Manufacturing Overhead Treatment:**

**Absorption Costing (Full Costing):**
• Fixed MOH is a PRODUCT cost
• Included in inventory
• Required by GAAP and tax regulations

**Variable Costing (Direct Costing):**
• Fixed MOH is a PERIOD cost
• Expensed immediately
• Used for internal decision-making

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Income Differences
sections.content:
**"PIE" - Production vs. Income Effect:**

**P**roduction > Sales → Absorption income **HIGHER**
(Fixed OH sits in inventory instead of expensing)

**I**nventory increases → Absorption income > Variable

**E**quals: When Production = Sales → Same income

**Simple rule:** If inventory goes UP, absorption > variable

sections.title: Reconciliation
sections.content:
**Income Difference Formula:**

Absorption Income - Variable Income = 
Change in Inventory (units) × Fixed MOH per unit

**From example:**
Inventory increase: 10,000 - 8,000 = 2,000 units
Fixed MOH/unit: $10
Difference: 2,000 × $10 = $20,000 ✓

**Works every time!**
Inventory ↑ → Absorption > Variable
Inventory ↓ → Variable > Absorption

sections.title: Advantages and Disadvantages
rows: GAAP compliant; matches costs to revenue; includes all product costs
Income can be manipulated by production levels; fixed costs capitalized
Better for CVP analysis; clear CM reporting; no production manipulation
Not GAAP; may undervalue inventory; fixed costs expensed immediately

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Manipulation via Production
sections.content:
**Under absorption costing, managers can boost income by overproducing!**

**Example:**
Manager needs $100,000 income but is at $80,000
Solution: Produce extra 2,000 units (Fixed MOH/unit = $10)
Result: $20,000 more fixed OH absorbed into inventory

**The units just sit in warehouse, but income goes up!**

**Variable costing eliminates this manipulation incentive.**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Absorption: Fixed MOH is product cost (GAAP required)
Variable: Fixed MOH is period cost (internal use)
Production > Sales → Absorption income higher
Production < Sales → Variable income higher
Production = Sales → Same income
Difference = Change in inventory × Fixed MOH/unit
Variable costing prevents production manipulation of income

sections.title: Absorption vs. Variable Costing: Which Method?

knowledgeCheck.question: A company produced 12,000 units and sold 9,000 units. Under which costing method would the company report higher net operating income if fixed manufacturing overhead costs are significant?
options: Both methods would report the same income
Cannot be determined without more information
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Absorption costing includes fixed manufacturing overhead in the cost of goods sold. When production exceeds sales, some of the fixed overhead remains in ending inventory, resulting in a lower cost of goods sold and higher net operating income compared to variable costing.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how fixed manufacturing overhead is treated under each method and what happens when production exceeds sales.

reveal.question: Which costing method is required for external financial reporting under Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)?
reveal.answer: Absorption costing is required for external financial reporting under GAAP. Variable costing is primarily for internal decision-making.

rows.label: Fixed Manufacturing Overhead
values: Product Cost (Included in Inventory)
Period Cost (Expensed Immediately)

rows.label: Income Statement Format
values: Traditional (Sales - COGS = Gross Profit)
Contribution Margin (Sales - Variable Costs = Contribution Margin)

rows.label: Used For
values: External Reporting, Pricing Decisions
Internal Decision-Making, Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: Using the information from the example (Selling price: $50/unit, Variable costs: $30/unit, Fixed MOH: $100,000, Fixed S&A: $50,000, Production: 10,000 units, Sales: 8,000 units), what is the dollar amount of fixed manufacturing overhead included in ending inventory under absorption costing?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Production was 10,000 units and sales were 8,000 units, meaning 2,000 units remain in ending inventory. The fixed manufacturing overhead rate is $10 per unit ($100,000 / 10,000 units). Therefore, the fixed manufacturing overhead in ending inventory is 2,000 units * $10/unit = $20,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the number of units in ending inventory and the fixed MOH per unit.

title: Joint and By-Product Costing
description: Allocate joint costs and account for by-products in manufacturing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Many industries produce multiple products from the same input (oil refining, meat processing, chemicals). CMAs must allocate joint costs for inventory valuation AND know that joint costs are irrelevant for sell-or-process-further decisions!

sections.title: Joint Cost Terminology
sections.content:
**Joint Costs:**
Costs incurred before products become separately identifiable.

**Split-off Point:**
Where joint products become separately identifiable.

**Joint Products:**
Main products from a joint process (each has significant value).

**By-Products:**
Minor products with relatively small value.

**Separable Costs:**
Costs incurred AFTER split-off (one product only).

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Joint Costs and Decisions
sections.content:
**"SUNK at Split-off"**

Joint costs are SUNK for sell-or-process-further decisions!

**Decision rule:**
Process further if:
Incremental Revenue > Incremental (Separable) Costs

**Joint costs are irrelevant - they're the same either way!**

Allocation of joint costs matters only for:
• Inventory valuation
• Cost-based pricing
• NOT for process-further decisions

sections.title: Sell vs. Process Further
sections.content:
**Product A at Split-off:**
• Sales value at split-off: $50,000
• Sales value after processing: $80,000
• Further processing costs: $20,000

**Analysis:**
Incremental revenue: $80,000 - $50,000 = $30,000
Incremental cost: $20,000
Incremental profit: **$10,000**

**Decision:** Process further!

**Note:** Joint costs of $60,000 allocated to Product A are IRRELEVANT - same either way.

sections.title: By-Product Accounting
sections.content:
**Two methods:**

**Production Method:**
• Recognize revenue when produced
• Credit Inventory or reduce COGS

**Sales Method:**
• Recognize revenue when sold
• Credit Other Revenue or reduce COGS

**Common treatments:**
• NRV of by-product reduces joint cost to allocate
• By-product revenue shown as Other Income
• By-product revenue reduces COGS

**By-products receive NO allocation of joint costs!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Joint costs incurred before split-off; separable costs after
NRV Method: Allocate based on (Final Sales - Separable Costs)
Joint costs are SUNK for sell-or-process decisions
Process further if Incremental Revenue > Incremental Cost
By-products: minor value, no joint cost allocation
Joint cost allocation for inventory valuation only
Physical measures appropriate when products are similar

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following best describes the 'split-off point' in a joint costing process?
options: The point at which joint costs are allocated to individual products.
The point where production begins for all joint products.
The point where joint products become separately identifiable.
The point at which by-products are sold.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The split-off point is the stage in the production process where the individual joint products can be distinguished and separated. Before this point, the costs incurred are joint costs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about when you can physically tell the products apart.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the Net Realizable Value (NRV) of a joint product?
reveal.answer: NRV = Final Sales Value - Separable Costs

knowledgeCheck.question: Using the NRV method, if a joint process results in Product X with a final sales value of $120,000 and separable costs of $30,000, and Product Y with a final sales value of $80,000 and separable costs of $10,000, what percentage of the total joint costs would be allocated to Product X?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the NRV for each product: Product X NRV = $120,000 - $30,000 = $90,000; Product Y NRV = $80,000 - $10,000 = $70,000. Total NRV = $90,000 + $70,000 = $160,000. Product X Allocation = ($90,000 / $160,000) = 56.25%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract separable costs from the final sales value before calculating the allocation percentage.

title: Standard Costing System
description: Establish and use standard costs for planning and control

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Standard costs are predetermined costs that serve as benchmarks. CMAs use standards for budgeting, variance analysis, and simplifying accounting. Standards help answer: "What SHOULD this product cost?" vs. "What DID it cost?"

sections.title: Setting Standards
sections.content:
**Materials Standards:**
• Quantity: Engineering specs + allowance for normal waste
• Price: Expected purchase price including freight, net of discounts

**Labor Standards:**
• Time: Time and motion studies + learning curve
• Rate: Expected wage rates including benefits, FICA

**Overhead Standards:**
• Variable: Based on cost driver relationship
• Fixed: Based on budgeted costs and normal capacity

**Involve operations, purchasing, HR in setting standards!**

sections.title: Standard Cost Card
sections.content:
**Product: Widget Model A**

```
Direct Materials:
  2 lbs @ $5.00/lb                    $10.00

Direct Labor:
  0.5 hrs @ $20.00/hr                 $10.00

Variable Overhead:
  0.5 DLH @ $6.00/DLH                  $3.00

Fixed Overhead:
  0.5 DLH @ $10.00/DLH                 $5.00

Standard Cost per Unit:               $28.00
```

**This becomes the "should cost" for planning and control.**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Standard Cost Benefits
sections.content:
**"PISC"** - Standards help with:

**P**lanning - Budgets based on standards
**I**nventory valuation - Simplify record-keeping
**S**implify accounting - Book at standard, track variances
**C**ontrol - Compare actual to standard, investigate variances

sections.title: Journal Entries with Standards
sections.content:
**Record at STANDARD, isolate VARIANCE:**

**Materials Purchase (at standard):**
Dr Raw Materials (AQ × SP)    10,500
Dr Materials Price Variance        420 U
    Cr Accounts Payable (AQ × AP)    10,920

**Materials Use (at standard):**
Dr WIP (SQ × SP)              10,000
Dr Materials Usage Variance      500 U
    Cr Raw Materials (AQ × SP)      10,500

**Variances are recorded AS THEY OCCUR!**

sections.title: Variance Investigation
sections.content:
**When to investigate:**
• Dollar threshold exceeded
• Percentage threshold exceeded
• Recurring pattern
• Controllable variance
• Statistical significance

**Cost-benefit test:**
Investigate if: Expected savings > Investigation cost

**Investigation options:**
• Internal review of processes
• Discuss with responsible managers
• Review source documents
• Statistical process control charts

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When to Update Standards
sections.content:
**Don't update standards too frequently!**

**Update when:**
• Permanent price changes occur
• Technology/process fundamentally changes
• Learning curve reaches steady state
• Original standard was wrong

**Don't update for:**
• Temporary fluctuations
• Seasonal variations
• One-time events

**Frequent changes make variance analysis meaningless!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Standards are predetermined benchmark costs
Currently attainable standards best for motivation
Standard cost card shows expected cost per unit
Record inventory at standard; isolate variances
Book variances as they occur, not at period end
Investigate material and recurring variances
Update standards for permanent changes only

knowledgeCheck.question: When setting the standard quantity of direct materials, which of the following should be included?
options: Engineering specifications plus an allowance for normal spoilage.
Engineering specifications only, as spoilage is considered abnormal and should be handled separately.
Ideal engineering specifications assuming no waste or spoilage.
Engineering specifications adjusted for the average historical spoilage rate, regardless of whether it is normal or abnormal.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The standard quantity of direct materials should include engineering specifications plus an allowance for normal waste or spoilage. Abnormal spoilage should be separately accounted for as it is not expected under normal operating conditions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what is 'expected' under normal conditions.

reveal.question: What are the primary methods used to determine the *time* component of the direct labor standard?
reveal.answer: Time and motion studies, considering the learning curve for new processes or employees. Time and motion studies analyze the efficiency of tasks, while the learning curve acknowledges that employees become more efficient over time as they gain experience.

rows.label: Focus
values: Expected purchase cost, net of discounts
Expected wage rates, including benefits & payroll taxes

rows.label: Key Considerations
values: Freight, purchase discounts, supplier reliability
Benefits, FICA, payroll taxes, skill level

title: Lean Manufacturing and JIT
description: Apply lean principles and just-in-time inventory management

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Lean manufacturing eliminates waste and reduces inventory. CMAs must understand how JIT changes costing, how to measure lean performance, and the risks/benefits of minimal inventory. This is increasingly tested as more companies adopt lean practices.

sections.title: The Seven Wastes (Muda)
sections.content:
**Lean identifies seven types of waste:**

1. **Overproduction** - Making more than needed
2. **Waiting** - Idle time, delays
3. **Transportation** - Unnecessary movement of materials
4. **Overprocessing** - More work than customer values
5. **Inventory** - Excess raw materials, WIP, finished goods
6. **Motion** - Unnecessary worker movement
7. **Defects** - Rework, scrap, inspection

**Goal: Eliminate all non-value-adding activities!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 7 Wastes
sections.content:
**"TIM WOOD"** - The 7 Wastes:

**T**ransportation
**I**nventory
**M**otion
**W**aiting
**O**verproduction
**O**verprocessing
**D**efects

**TIM WOOD is the enemy of lean!**

sections.title: Just-in-Time (JIT) Principles
sections.content:
**Core concepts:**
• **Pull system** - Produce only when demanded by next stage
• **Zero defects** - Quality at the source
• **Minimal inventory** - Material arrives just when needed
• **Continuous improvement** (Kaizen)
• **Respect for workers** - Empower problem-solving

**JIT requires:**
• Reliable suppliers
• Short setup times
• Flexible workforce
• High-quality processes

sections.title: Backflush Costing
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Costing system that delays recording until completion or sale.

**How it works:**
1. No WIP account (or minimal)
2. Costs "backflushed" when units completed or sold
3. Standard costs applied at that point
4. Variances recognized at completion

**Appropriate when:**
• JIT environment
• Minimal WIP at any time
• Short production cycles
• Stable, predictable processes

sections.title: Lean Performance Metrics
sections.content:
**Beyond traditional cost metrics:**

• **Lead time** - Order to delivery
• **Cycle time** - Start to finish for one unit
• **Setup time** - Time to change over
• **First-pass yield** - % with no rework
• **Throughput** - Units per period
• **Days of inventory** - Lower is better
• **Value-added ratio** - (Value-add time / Total time)

**Focus on TIME and QUALITY, not just cost!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: JIT Risks
sections.content:
**JIT isn't always appropriate:**

**Risks of minimal inventory:**
• Supply chain disruptions (COVID example!)
• Supplier quality problems
• Demand spikes can't be met
• Single-source supplier dependency

**JIT works best with:**
• Reliable suppliers
• Stable demand
• Short distances
• Healthy supplier relationships

**Exam may ask when JIT is NOT appropriate!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Seven wastes (TIM WOOD) are targets for elimination
JIT is a pull system - produce only when demanded
JIT requires minimal inventory, reliable suppliers
Backflush costing simplifies accounting in JIT
Lean metrics focus on time and quality, not just cost
JIT risks: supply disruptions, demand variability
Kaizen = continuous improvement

sections.title: Test Your Understanding of the Seven Wastes

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following activities would LEAST likely be categorized as 'Waiting' (one of the seven wastes) in a lean manufacturing environment?
options: A machine operator standing idle while a machine is being repaired.
Raw materials sitting in a warehouse awaiting use in production.
A batch of products sitting in a queue waiting to be processed by the next workstation.
A design engineer spending time refining a product feature that customers do not value.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Overprocessing refers to putting more effort into a product than the customer is willing to pay for. While the other options represent 'Waiting' or 'Inventory' waste, over-refining a product is Overprocessing.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which activity adds unnecessary cost without adding value to the customer.

reveal.question: What are the five core principles of Just-in-Time (JIT)?
reveal.answer: The five core principles of Just-in-Time (JIT) are: 1. Pull system, 2. Zero defects, 3. Minimal inventory, 4. Continuous improvement (Kaizen), and 5. Respect for workers.

rows.label: Production Trigger
values: Forecasted Demand
Actual Demand (next stage)

rows.label: Inventory Levels
values: Higher (buffer stock)
Lower (minimal WIP)

rows.label: Waste Emphasis
values: Focuses less on inventory waste
Designed to minimize all forms of waste

rows.label: Example
values: Traditional Manufacturing
Lean Manufacturing (JIT)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST critical requirement for the successful implementation of a Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory system?
options: Negotiating the lowest possible price with suppliers, even if it means longer lead times.
Maintaining a large safety stock of raw materials to buffer against unexpected demand surges.
Establishing strong, reliable relationships with suppliers to ensure timely and consistent delivery of materials.
Investing heavily in automated production equipment to maximize output and minimize labor costs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: JIT relies on a consistent and predictable supply chain. Strong supplier relationships are crucial to ensuring that materials arrive just when needed, minimizing inventory and avoiding production delays. The other options are contrary to JIT principles.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what is most important for materials arriving just in time.

title: Target Costing and Kaizen
description: Apply target costing for product development and kaizen for continuous improvement

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Target costing starts with the customer and works backward to allowable costs. CMAs drive cross-functional teams to design products that meet cost AND profit targets. This is essential for competitive industries where price is market-driven.

sections.title: Target Costing Formula
sections.content:
**Traditional approach:**
Price = Cost + Profit
(We ADD profit to cost)

**Target costing approach:**
Target Cost = Target Price - Required Profit
(We SUBTRACT from price)

**The market sets the price!**

**Example:**
• Market research shows customers will pay: $100
• Required profit margin: 20%
• Required profit: $100 × 20% = $20
• **Target Cost: $100 - $20 = $80**

Now design the product to cost $80 or less!

sections.title: Target Costing Process
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Conduct market research (what will customers pay?)

**Step 2:** Determine target selling price

**Step 3:** Calculate required profit margin

**Step 4:** Calculate target cost (Price - Profit)

**Step 5:** Design product to meet target cost
• Value engineering
• Supplier involvement
• Design for manufacturability

**Step 6:** If cost can't be achieved, don't launch product!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Target Costing
sections.content:
**"The Market is the Boss"**

Traditional: Cost → Price (cost-plus)
• We tell the market our price

Target: Price → Cost (market-driven)
• Market tells us the price
• We work backward to cost

**Formula: Target Cost = Target Price - Target Profit**

sections.title: Value Engineering
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Systematic analysis to achieve required functions at lowest cost.

**Key question:** What does the customer actually VALUE?

**Techniques:**
• Eliminate unnecessary features
• Simplify design
• Use cheaper materials (same quality)
• Reduce number of parts
• Design for manufacturing
• Involve suppliers early

**Value = Function / Cost**
Increase value by improving function OR reducing cost!

sections.title: Kaizen Costing
sections.content:
**Target costing:** BEFORE production (design phase)
**Kaizen costing:** DURING production (ongoing)

**Kaizen = Continuous Improvement**

**Approach:**
• Set cost reduction targets each period
• Small, incremental improvements
• Involve all employees
• Focus on process improvements

**Example:**
Year 1 actual cost: $82
Kaizen target: Reduce by 3% per year
Year 2 target: $82 × 97% = $79.54

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Target Costing Prerequisites
sections.content:
**Target costing works best when:**

• Market price is competitive/known
• Design flexibility exists
• Cross-functional teams available
• Supplier collaboration possible
• Long product development cycle

**Less effective when:**
• You have pricing power (monopoly)
• Design is fixed (commodities)
• Short development cycles
• Limited supplier options

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Target Cost = Target Price - Required Profit
Market determines price; we design to cost
Value engineering reduces cost while maintaining function
Target costing: design phase; Kaizen: production phase
80% of costs are committed in design phase
Life-cycle costing considers R&D through disposal
Kaizen = continuous small improvements

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is developing a new product. Market research indicates a target selling price of $150 per unit. The company requires a profit margin of 30% of sales. What is the target cost per unit that the company must achieve?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The target cost is calculated as Target Selling Price - Required Profit. In this case, $150 - ($150 * 0.30) = $150 - $45 = $105.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the profit amount based on the selling price before subtracting.

reveal.question: What is the primary objective of value engineering?
reveal.answer: To achieve required functions at the lowest possible cost, by systematically analyzing the value of each component and function of a product or service.

rows.label: Price Determination
values: Price = Cost + Profit
Target Cost = Target Price - Required Profit

rows.label: Market Influence
values: Cost determines price, less market influence
Market determines price, heavy market influence

rows.label: Starting Point
values: Starts with cost
Starts with price

title: Supply Chain Management
description: Understand supply chain concepts and their cost management implications

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Supply chain costs can exceed direct production costs! CMAs analyze total cost of ownership, evaluate make-vs-buy decisions, and measure supplier performance. Poor supply chain management destroys value; excellent SCM creates competitive advantage.

sections.title: Supply Chain Components
sections.content:
**Upstream (Inbound):**
• Suppliers and their suppliers
• Procurement
• Inbound logistics

**Operations:**
• Manufacturing/conversion
• Internal logistics

**Downstream (Outbound):**
• Distribution
• Wholesalers/retailers
• End customers

**Goal: Optimize the ENTIRE chain, not just your piece!**

sections.title: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
sections.content:
**TCO includes more than purchase price:**

**Acquisition costs:**
• Purchase price
• Ordering costs
• Freight and duties

**Possession costs:**
• Carrying costs
• Storage and handling
• Insurance

**Usage costs:**
• Quality issues (inspection, rework)
• Downtime from defects
• Warranty claims

**Lowest price ≠ Lowest total cost!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: TCO
sections.content:
**"APU"** - Three cost categories:

**A**cquisition - Getting it
**P**ossession - Holding it
**U**sage - Using it

**Example:**
Supplier A: $10/unit + $2 shipping + $1 inspection = $13 TCO
Supplier B: $11/unit + $1 shipping + $0 inspection = $12 TCO

**Higher price, lower TCO wins!**

sections.title: Make vs. Buy Decision
sections.content:
**Relevant costs to compare:**

**Make:**
• Variable manufacturing costs
• Direct fixed costs (avoidable)
• Opportunity cost of capacity

**Buy:**
• Purchase price
• Ordering costs
• Receiving and inspection
• Quality assurance

**Exclude:** Allocated fixed costs (not avoidable)

**Qualitative factors:**
• Control over quality
• Confidentiality/IP protection
• Supplier reliability
• Strategic importance

sections.title: Supplier Selection and Management
sections.content:
**Vendor evaluation criteria:**
• Quality (defect rates, certifications)
• Delivery (on-time %, lead time)
• Price and payment terms
• Capacity and flexibility
• Financial stability
• Innovation capability
• Sustainability/ethical practices

**Supplier relationship spectrum:**
• Arms-length (multiple sources, price focus)
• Preferred supplier (fewer, longer-term)
• Strategic partner (integrated, shared planning)
• Vertical integration (ownership)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Outsourcing Risks
sections.content:
**Don't outsource purely for cost savings!**

**Hidden risks:**
• Loss of core competency
• Quality control challenges
• Supply disruption (single source)
• Intellectual property exposure
• Hidden costs (coordination, travel)
• Currency and political risk (offshore)

**Strategic items should usually stay in-house!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Supply chain: upstream (suppliers) → operations → downstream (customers)
TCO = Acquisition + Possession + Usage costs
Lowest price doesn't mean lowest TCO
Make vs. Buy: Use only relevant (avoidable) costs
Exclude allocated fixed costs from make/buy analysis
Consider qualitative factors in sourcing decisions
Outsourcing has hidden costs and strategic risks

sections.title: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following costs would NOT be included when calculating the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) of a purchased component?
options: Costs associated with inspecting the component for defects.
Allocated fixed overhead costs for the purchasing department.
Costs of storing the component before it is used in production.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Allocated fixed overhead costs are generally considered irrelevant for decision-making because they will be incurred regardless of whether the component is purchased. TCO focuses on costs directly attributable to the acquisition, possession, and usage of the asset.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which costs are avoidable and directly related to owning and using the component.

reveal.question: List the three main stages of a supply chain.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is deciding whether to make or buy a component. The variable manufacturing cost to make the component is $10 per unit. Direct fixed costs that can be avoided if the component is bought are $5 per unit. The purchase price is $18 per unit. Allocated fixed overhead is $3 per unit. What is the relevant cost to MAKE the component?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The relevant cost to make the component is the sum of the variable manufacturing cost ($10) and the avoidable direct fixed costs ($5), totaling $15. Allocated fixed overhead is irrelevant.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to only include costs that are avoidable and directly related to the 'make' decision.

title: Cost Estimation Methods
description: Learn quantitative techniques for estimating costs and separating fixed from variable components

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Accurate cost estimation is essential for budgeting, pricing, and decision-making! CMAs must separate fixed and variable costs to forecast future costs, analyze cost behavior, and support strategic decisions. These techniques are heavily tested on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Cost Estimation Approaches
rows: Classify each account as fixed or variable based on judgment
Use highest and lowest activity points to calculate variable rate
Plot costs against activity and visually fit a line
Statistical technique using all data points

sections.title: The High-Low Method
sections.content:
**Step-by-Step Process:**

**Step 1:** Identify highest and lowest activity levels

**Step 2:** Calculate variable cost per unit
Variable Rate = (Cost at High - Cost at Low) / (High Activity - Low Activity)

**Step 3:** Calculate fixed cost
Fixed Cost = Total Cost - (Variable Rate × Activity)

**Example:**
High: 10,000 units, $80,000 total cost
Low: 6,000 units, $56,000 total cost

Variable Rate = ($80,000 - $56,000) / (10,000 - 6,000)
= $24,000 / 4,000 = $6 per unit

Fixed Cost = $80,000 - ($6 × 10,000) = $20,000

**Cost Formula:** Y = $20,000 + $6X

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: High-Low Steps
sections.content:
**"HIgh LOw = HILO"**

**H**ighest and **L**owest points identified
**I**ncremental cost calculated (cost change)
**L**evel of activity change determined
**O**utput: Variable rate = Cost change / Activity change

Then: Fixed = Total - (Variable × Units)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: High-Low Limitations
sections.content:
**The high-low method has significant limitations:**

1. **Uses only 2 data points** - ignores all other observations
2. **Outliers** - if high or low point is unusual, results are skewed
3. **Assumes linearity** - relationship must be linear
4. **Non-representative** - extreme points may not reflect normal operations

**Best practice:** Use regression when data is available; high-low for quick estimates only.

sections.title: Regression Analysis
sections.content:
**Linear Regression Formula:**
Y = a + bX

Where:
• Y = Total cost (dependent variable)
• a = Fixed cost (Y-intercept)
• b = Variable cost per unit (slope)
• X = Activity level (independent variable)

**Advantages over High-Low:**
• Uses ALL data points
• Provides statistical measures of reliability
• Identifies outliers and anomalies

**Key Statistics:**
• **R² (Coefficient of Determination):** % of cost variation explained by activity
• **Standard Error:** Average prediction error
• **t-statistic:** Tests if coefficients are statistically significant

sections.title: Interpreting R-Squared
rows: Excellent fit - activity explains most cost variation
Moderate fit - other factors affect costs
Weak fit - look for better cost drivers

sections.title: Learning Curve Analysis
sections.content:
**Concept:** Labor time decreases as workers gain experience

**Learning Curve Effect:**
Each time cumulative production doubles, time per unit decreases by a constant percentage.

**Example - 80% Learning Curve:**
• 1st unit: 100 hours
• 2nd unit (cumulative avg): 80 hours (100 × 0.80)
• 4th unit (cumulative avg): 64 hours (80 × 0.80)
• 8th unit (cumulative avg): 51.2 hours (64 × 0.80)

**Applications:**
• Bidding on new contracts
• Setting labor standards
• Planning production capacity
• Pricing new products

sections.title: Learning Curve Formula
sections.content:
**Cumulative Average Time Model:**
Y = aX^b

Where:
• Y = Cumulative average time per unit
• a = Time for first unit
• X = Cumulative number of units
• b = Learning coefficient (log of learning rate / log 2)

**For 80% Learning Curve:**
b = log(0.80) / log(2) = -0.322

**Key Insight:** Lower learning rate % = Faster learning
• 70% curve: Very fast learning (complex assembly)
• 90% curve: Slow learning (simple tasks)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cost estimation separates fixed and variable components for forecasting
High-low method uses only highest and lowest activity points
High-low: Variable Rate = Change in Cost / Change in Activity
Regression analysis uses all data points and is more accurate
R² measures how well activity explains cost variation
Learning curves show how labor time decreases with experience
Each doubling of production reduces time by the learning curve percentage

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's highest production was 8,000 units with costs of $52,000. The lowest was 3,000 units with costs of $27,000. Using high-low, what is the variable cost per unit?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Divide the change in cost by the change in activity.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has an 80% learning curve. The first unit took 100 hours. What is the cumulative average time per unit after producing 4 units?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With an 80% learning curve, each doubling reduces time to 80% of previous. 1 unit = 100 hours, 2 units = 80 hours, 4 units = 64 hours (80 × 0.80).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Count the doublings: 1→2→4 = two doublings.

reveal.question: What are the main limitations of the high-low method?
reveal.answer: The high-low method: (1) Uses only two data points, ignoring all other observations, (2) Is sensitive to outliers if extreme points are unusual, (3) Assumes a perfectly linear relationship, and (4) May not represent normal operations if extreme points are anomalies.

title: Process Costing Systems
description: Master the fundamentals of process costing for continuous production environments

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Process costing is essential for industries like chemicals, food processing, petroleum, and pharmaceuticals where products flow continuously through standardized processes. CMAs must understand equivalent unit calculations and cost flow assumptions!

sections.title: Job Costing vs Process Costing
rows: Total job cost ÷ units in job

sections.title: Equivalent Units of Production (EUP)
sections.content:
**The Core Concept:** Converting partially completed units to equivalent whole units

**Why Needed:**
• At period end, some units are partially complete
• Can't just count physical units
• Need to express work done in terms of complete units

**Formula:**
Equivalent Units = Physical Units × Percentage Complete

**Example:**
10,000 units that are 40% complete
= 10,000 × 40% = 4,000 equivalent units

**Key Point:** EUP is calculated separately for:
• Direct materials (often added at specific point)
• Conversion costs (usually applied evenly)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EUP Steps
sections.content:
**"SAUCE" - Process Costing Steps:**

**S**ummarize physical unit flow
**A**ccumulate costs to account for
**U**nit calculation (equivalent units)
**C**ost per equivalent unit
**E**valuate ending inventory & COGM

Always start with physical units, then convert to equivalent units!

sections.title: Weighted Average Method
sections.content:
**Approach:** Mixes beginning inventory costs with current period costs

**EUP Calculation:**
EUP = Units transferred out + (Ending WIP × % complete)

**Cost per EUP:**
= (Beginning WIP costs + Current period costs) ÷ EUP

**Example:**
Beginning WIP: 5,000 units, $15,000
Started: 45,000 units
Completed and transferred: 40,000 units
Ending WIP: 10,000 units (60% complete for conversion)

EUP = 40,000 + (10,000 × 60%) = 46,000

**Advantage:** Simple to calculate
**Disadvantage:** Blends periods, less accurate for performance evaluation

sections.title: FIFO Method
sections.content:
**Approach:** Separates beginning inventory from current period work

**EUP Calculation:**
EUP = Work to complete beginning WIP
     + Units started and completed
     + Work on ending WIP

**Same Example with FIFO:**
Beginning WIP: 5,000 units (40% complete)

EUP = (5,000 × 60%)        Work to complete beginning
    + 35,000               Started & completed
    + (10,000 × 60%)       Work on ending WIP
    = 3,000 + 35,000 + 6,000 = 44,000

**Advantage:** Better for performance evaluation (current period only)
**Disadvantage:** More complex calculation

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Materials Added at Different Points
sections.content:
**Watch for: When materials are added**

**Added at Beginning:** All units in WIP are 100% complete for materials

**Added at End:** Only completed units have materials (0% for WIP)

**Added Evenly:** Same as conversion costs

**Example Trap:**
Ending WIP: 8,000 units, 50% complete
Materials added at beginning
Conversion added evenly

EUP for materials: 8,000 (100%)
EUP for conversion: 4,000 (50%)

**Exam often tests different % for materials vs conversion!

sections.title: Cost Reconciliation
sections.content:
**The Final Step:** Account for all costs

**Costs to Account For:**
• Beginning WIP costs
• Current period costs added

**Costs Accounted For:**
• Transferred out (units × cost per EUP)
• Ending WIP inventory
  - Materials: EUP × cost per unit
  - Conversion: EUP × cost per unit

**These Must Equal!**

**Check Your Work:**
Total costs to account for = Costs accounted for
(If not equal, calculation error somewhere)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Process costing is for continuous, homogeneous production
Equivalent units convert partial completion to whole unit equivalents
Calculate EUP separately for materials and conversion costs
Weighted average blends periods; FIFO separates current period work
FIFO is better for performance evaluation
Watch for when materials are added (beginning, end, or evenly)
Cost reconciliation must balance: costs in = costs out

knowledgeCheck.question: A department completed and transferred 30,000 units during the month. Ending work-in-process consists of 8,000 units that are 100% complete for materials and 75% complete for conversion. Using the weighted average method, what are the equivalent units for conversion costs?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: EUP for conversion = Units transferred out + (Ending WIP × % complete) = 30,000 + (8,000 × 75%) = 30,000 + 6,000 = 36,000 equivalent units.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Under weighted average, EUP includes all completed units plus the equivalent work in ending inventory.

sections.title: Why Use FIFO Over Weighted Average?

reveal.question: In what situations would FIFO process costing be preferred over weighted average?
reveal.answer: FIFO is preferred when: (1) Costs fluctuate significantly between periods, (2) Management wants to evaluate current period performance separately from prior periods, (3) Beginning inventory is large relative to current production, and (4) More accurate cost information is needed for pricing and cost control decisions.

title: Transfer Pricing Strategies
description: Set internal prices that promote goal congruence and optimal decision-making

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Transfer prices can make or break divisional performance evaluation! Set too high, the buying division looks unprofitable. Set too low, the selling division loses. CMAs must design transfer pricing policies that promote good decisions for the company as a whole.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Definition:** The price charged when one segment of a company provides goods or services to another segment

**Why It Matters:**
• Affects divisional profit measurement
• Influences manager decisions
• Tax implications for international transfers

**The Challenge:**
Set price so that:
• Selling division is fairly compensated
• Buying division pays a reasonable cost
• Both divisions make decisions good for the company

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Methods
rows: External market price for identical product
Active market exists; divisions are independent
Variable cost, full cost, or cost-plus
No external market; internal only product
Some external market; divisions have options
Different price for buyer and seller
Conflicts can't be resolved with single price

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Market Price Rule
sections.content:
**"Market is Best" - When it exists!**

**Market-based transfer pricing:**
• Promotes goal congruence
• Divisional autonomy preserved
• Fair to both parties
• Matches external opportunity cost

**The Golden Standard:**
If seller could sell externally at $100
If buyer could buy externally at $100
Transfer price should be around $100

sections.title: Cost-Based Methods
sections.content:
**Variable Cost:**
• Minimum transfer price
• Good for decisions
• Problem: Seller shows no profit

**Full Cost:**
• Variable + allocated fixed costs
• Covers all costs
• Problem: Fixed cost allocation is arbitrary

**Cost-Plus:**
• Full cost + markup for profit
• Seller earns profit
• Problem: Markup is arbitrary

**Issue with All Cost Methods:**
Selling division has no incentive to control costs!
(Costs just get passed along)

sections.title: The General Transfer Pricing Rule
sections.content:
**Optimal Transfer Price:**

**Minimum TP = Variable Cost + Opportunity Cost**

**Where Opportunity Cost =**
• Lost contribution margin from external sales
• $0 if seller has excess capacity
• Full margin if seller is at capacity

**Example - Seller at Capacity:**
Variable cost: $30
External selling price: $50
Opportunity cost: $50 - $30 = $20

Minimum TP = $30 + $20 = $50 (market price)

**Example - Seller with Excess Capacity:**
Variable cost: $30
Opportunity cost: $0 (no lost sales)

Minimum TP = $30 + $0 = $30

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Range of Acceptable Prices
sections.content:
**For transfers to occur, TP must be in the range:**

**Minimum (Seller):** Variable cost + Opportunity cost
**Maximum (Buyer):** Market price (what buyer would pay externally)

**If Minimum > Maximum:** No internal transfer makes sense!
• Seller better off selling externally
• Buyer better off buying externally

**Exam Calculation:**
1. Calculate minimum TP (seller's perspective)
2. Calculate maximum TP (buyer's perspective)
3. If range exists, transfer should occur
4. If no range, external transactions preferred

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Transfer prices affect divisional profits and manager decisions
Market-based pricing is best when external market exists
Cost-based pricing used when no external market exists
General rule: TP ≥ Variable cost + Opportunity cost
Excess capacity means opportunity cost = $0
At full capacity, opportunity cost = lost contribution margin
Transfer should occur only if acceptable range exists

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A produces a component with a variable cost of $40 and sells it externally for $65. Division A is operating at full capacity. What is the minimum transfer price Division A should accept for internal sales to Division B?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: At full capacity, the minimum TP = Variable cost + Opportunity cost = $40 + ($65 - $40) = $40 + $25 = $65. The opportunity cost is the full contribution margin from external sales that would be lost.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When at full capacity, what does Division A give up by selling internally?

title: Spoilage, Rework, and Scrap Accounting
description: Account for production losses and inefficiencies in manufacturing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Production is never 100% efficient! Understanding how to account for spoilage, scrap, and rework is essential for accurate product costing and identifying improvement opportunities. The treatment differs based on whether losses are normal or abnormal.

sections.title: Key Terms Defined
sections.content:
**Spoilage:**
• Units that fail to meet quality standards
• Cannot be reworked economically
• Either discarded or sold at salvage value

**Rework:**
• Defective units that CAN be corrected
• Additional processing to meet standards
• Adds cost but saves the unit

**Scrap:**
• Material left over from production
• Cuttings, turnings, shavings
• Has some residual value
• Cannot be reworked into product

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Normal vs Abnormal
sections.content:
**"Normal = Product, Abnormal = Period"**

**Normal Spoilage:**
• "Cost of doing business"
• Becomes part of inventory cost
• Flows to COGS when sold

**Abnormal Spoilage:**
• "Unexpected failure"
• Loss on income statement
• Expensed immediately

**Think of it this way:**
Normal = Built into the product price
Abnormal = Management's problem (a loss!)

sections.title: Calculating Normal Spoilage
sections.content:
**Formula:**

Normal Spoilage = Good Output × Normal Spoilage Rate

**Example:**
• Normal spoilage rate: 5%
• Good units produced: 10,000
• Total spoiled: 700 units

Normal spoilage = 10,000 × 5% = 500 units
Abnormal spoilage = 700 - 500 = 200 units

**Accounting:**
• 500 units cost → Added to good units
• 200 units cost → Loss on income statement

**Net Salvage:**
• Deduct salvage value from spoilage cost
• Net cost is what gets allocated or expensed

sections.title: Accounting for Scrap
sections.content:
**Two Approaches:**

**Method 1: Reduce Product Cost**
• When scrap is traced to specific job
• Credit WIP for scrap value
• Reduces cost of that job

**Method 2: Other Income**
• When scrap is common to all production
• Credit "Scrap Revenue" (other income)
• OR credit Factory Overhead Control

**Timing:**
• Recognize when scrap is produced (if material)
• Or when scrap is sold (if immaterial)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rework Costs
sections.content:
**Normal Rework (inherent to process):**
• Charge to factory overhead
• Spread across all production
• Part of standard cost

**Normal Rework (specific job/customer specs):**
• Charge to specific job
• Customer effectively pays for rework

**Abnormal Rework (due to error):**
• Charge to Period Expense (Loss)
• NOT included in product cost

**Key Question:** Was rework EXPECTED?
Yes → Product cost
No → Period expense

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Spoilage = failed units; Scrap = leftover materials; Rework = fixable defects
Normal spoilage is expected and becomes product cost
Abnormal spoilage is unexpected and becomes period expense
Normal spoilage = Good output × Normal rate
Excess over normal = abnormal spoilage
Scrap reduces product cost or is recorded as other income
Rework treatment depends on whether it's expected or not

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a 3% normal spoilage rate. It produced 8,000 good units and had 400 spoiled units. How should the spoilage be accounted for?
options: 240 units to product cost, 160 units to period expense
200 units to product cost, 200 units to period expense
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Normal spoilage = 8,000 good units × 3% = 240 units. These 240 units' costs are added to good units (product cost). The remaining 400 - 240 = 160 units are abnormal spoilage and charged to period expense.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate normal spoilage using the rate applied to GOOD output, then the excess is abnormal.

title: Throughput Accounting and Theory of Constraints
description: Apply TOC principles to maximize throughput and profitability

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional costing spreads overhead everywhere - but TOC focuses on what REALLY limits profits! Understanding bottlenecks and throughput helps CMAs make better product mix and investment decisions. This is a favorite CMA exam topic!

sections.title: Theory of Constraints (TOC) Basics
sections.content:
**Core Concept:**
Every system has at least one constraint (bottleneck)
That constraint determines system throughput

**TOC Philosophy:**
• Focus ALL attention on the constraint
• Don't optimize non-constraints (waste)
• One hour lost at bottleneck = one hour lost forever
• One hour saved at non-bottleneck = mirage

**The Goal:**
Maximize THROUGHPUT, not utilization
Profit = Throughput - Operating Expenses

sections.title: TOC Five Focusing Steps
sections.content:
**Step 1: IDENTIFY the constraint**
• What limits output?
• Could be machine, person, policy, market

**Step 2: EXPLOIT the constraint**
• Get maximum output from existing constraint
• No idle time at bottleneck

**Step 3: SUBORDINATE everything else**
• Non-constraints serve the constraint
• Don't overproduce at non-bottlenecks

**Step 4: ELEVATE the constraint**
• Invest to increase constraint capacity
• Add shifts, equipment, training

**Step 5: REPEAT**
• Constraint may move!
• Start over at Step 1

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Throughput Accounting Measures
sections.content:
**"TIO" - The Three TOC Measures:**

**T**hroughput = Revenue - Truly Variable Costs
(Only raw materials - NOT labor!)

**I**nventory = Money tied up in things to sell
(All inventory at material cost only)

**O**perating Expense = Everything else
(All labor, overhead, fixed costs)

**Profit = Throughput - Operating Expense**

**Key Difference from Traditional:**
Labor is NOT variable in TOC - it's fixed!

sections.title: Product Mix with Constraint
sections.content:
**When bottleneck capacity is limited:**

**DON'T** rank by contribution margin
**DO** rank by throughput per constraint unit

**Formula:**
Throughput per Constraint Hour
= (Price - Materials) / Constraint Hours

**Example:**
|Product|Price|Materials|Throughput|Constraint Hrs|
|A|$100|$30|$70|2 hrs|
|B|$80|$20|$60|1 hr|

**Traditional:** Product A has higher CM ($70 > $60)
**TOC:** Product B has higher throughput/hour ($60 > $35)

**Prioritize B when bottleneck is limiting!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Drum-Buffer-Rope
sections.content:
**TOC scheduling system:**

**Drum:** The constraint sets the pace
(Like a drum beat - everyone follows)

**Buffer:** Protective inventory before constraint
(Never let constraint starve for work)

**Rope:** Communication back to start
(Only release material as fast as drum beats)

**Purpose:**
• Prevent WIP buildup
• Maximize constraint utilization
• Reduce inventory and lead times

**Key Concept:** DON'T maximize machine utilization everywhere - only at constraint!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Constraint (bottleneck) determines system throughput
Five steps: Identify, Exploit, Subordinate, Elevate, Repeat
Throughput = Revenue - Truly Variable Costs (materials only)
Rank product mix by throughput per constraint hour
Traditional CM ranking fails when constraint is binding
Drum-Buffer-Rope: schedule based on constraint pace
Don't optimize non-constraints - it creates excess inventory

knowledgeCheck.question: Machine X is a bottleneck with 100 hours available. Product P contributes $120 and uses 4 bottleneck hours. Product Q contributes $100 and uses 2 bottleneck hours. Which product should be prioritized?
options: Product Q (higher throughput per bottleneck hour)
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Product P: $120 / 4 hrs = $30/hr. Product Q: $100 / 2 hrs = $50/hr. Even though P has higher CM ($120 vs $100), Q generates more throughput per scarce constraint hour. Prioritize Q!
knowledgeCheck.hint: When a bottleneck constrains output, maximize throughput per constraint hour, not total contribution margin.

title: Joint Product and By-Product Costing
description: Allocate joint costs and account for by-products in process manufacturing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: What happens when one process creates multiple products? CMAs must allocate joint costs for inventory valuation and profitability analysis. Warning: Joint cost allocations are ARBITRARY - don't use them for sell-or-process decisions!

sections.title: Key Terminology
sections.content:
**Joint Costs:**
Costs incurred up to split-off point
Cannot be traced to individual products
Must be allocated

**Split-Off Point:**
Where joint products become separately identifiable
Where allocation decisions are made

**Joint Products:**
• Multiple products of significant value
• Result from same process
• Neither can be produced without others

**By-Products:**
• Minor product with little value
• Incidental to main products

**Separable Costs:**
Costs incurred AFTER split-off
Can be traced to specific products

sections.title: Joint Cost Allocation Methods
rows: Products with similar value per unit

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sales Value Method
sections.content:
**Most intuitive approach:**

**Step 1:** Find sales value at split-off for each product

**Step 2:** Calculate % of total sales value
Product A / Total Sales Value = A%

**Step 3:** Allocate joint costs by %
Joint Costs × A% = A's allocated cost

**Example:**
• Joint Costs: $100,000
• Product A: $60,000 at split-off (60%)
• Product B: $40,000 at split-off (40%)
• A gets: $60,000 of costs
• B gets: $40,000 of costs

sections.title: Net Realizable Value Method
sections.content:
**When products are processed further:**

**NRV = Final Sales Value - Separable Costs**

**Example:**
| Product | Final Value | Separable | NRV |
|---------|------------|-----------|-----|
| A | $80,000 | $20,000 | $60,000 |
| B | $70,000 | $30,000 | $40,000 |
| Total | | | $100,000 |

**Joint Costs: $80,000**

**Allocation:**
• A: 60% × $80,000 = $48,000
• B: 40% × $80,000 = $32,000

**Why NRV?**
Reflects actual value created by each product
Avoids penalizing products requiring more processing

sections.title: By-Product Accounting
sections.content:
**Two Methods:**

**Method 1: Reduce Joint Cost (Production Method)**
• Recognize revenue when by-product is produced
• NRV of by-product reduces joint costs
• Main products bear lower costs

**Method 2: Revenue at Sale (Sales Method)**
• Recognize revenue when by-product is sold
• Treated as Other Revenue or reduction to COGS
• Simpler, no inventory carrying for by-product

**Practical Approach:**
By-products are often treated as reducing joint costs
avoids tracking minimal-value inventory

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Decision Relevance
sections.content:
**CRITICAL: Joint costs are IRRELEVANT for:**

**Sell-or-Process-Further Decision:**
• Joint costs are sunk at split-off
• Compare: Additional revenue vs Separable costs
• Ignore allocated joint costs!

**Correct Analysis:**
• Incremental revenue from processing
• Minus incremental (separable) costs
• If positive: Process further
• If negative: Sell at split-off

**Why?**
Joint costs are incurred regardless of what you do with products after split-off

**Exam loves this trap!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Joint costs occur before split-off and must be allocated
Methods: Physical units, Sales value, NRV, Constant GM%
Sales value at split-off is most common method
NRV = Final value - Separable costs (for further processed products)
By-products: Reduce joint cost or recognize revenue at sale
CRITICAL: Joint costs are irrelevant for sell-or-process decisions
Sell-or-process: Compare incremental revenue to separable costs only

knowledgeCheck.question: Product X can sell at split-off for $50,000 or be processed further for $20,000 in additional costs and sold for $75,000. Joint cost allocated to X is $30,000. Should X be processed further?
options: No, because total profit would only be $25,000
No, because processing cost exceeds allocated joint cost
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Ignore joint costs (sunk). Incremental revenue: $75,000 - $50,000 = $25,000. Incremental cost: $20,000. Incremental profit: $5,000 positive. Process further! The $30,000 joint cost is irrelevant.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Joint costs are sunk at split-off. Only compare incremental revenues and costs.

title: Transfer Pricing Methods
description: Set transfer prices that optimize divisional performance and company-wide profits

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: When divisions trade with each other, at what price? Transfer pricing affects divisional profits, manager incentives, and overall company performance. CMAs must set prices that motivate good decisions across the organization.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Objectives
sections.content:
**Conflicting Goals:**

**Goal Congruence:**
• Divisions should make decisions good for company overall
• Accept all transactions with positive company contribution

**Performance Evaluation:**
• Each division's profit should reflect its contribution
• Fair measurement for bonus purposes

**Autonomy:**
• Divisional managers should have freedom
• Make-or-buy decisions locally
• No forced internal sourcing

**Challenge:**
No single price achieves all objectives perfectly!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Optimal Transfer Price
sections.content:
**General Rule (Minimum Transfer Price):**

**TP = Variable Cost + Opportunity Cost**

**Opportunity Cost:**
• If no excess capacity = Lost contribution margin
• If excess capacity = $0 (no lost sales)

**Examples:**

**Excess capacity:**
TP = $10 variable cost + $0 = **$10**

**Full capacity, can sell externally:**
External price = $25, Variable cost = $10
TP = $10 + ($25 - $10) = **$25** (market price!)

**Insight:** With perfect market, market price is optimal

sections.title: Method Analysis
sections.content:
**Market Price:**
+ Best for goal congruence
+ Fair performance evaluation
- Requires competitive market
- May not exist for specialized products

**Variable Cost:**
+ Simple
+ Good for decisions with excess capacity
- Selling division shows no profit
- May understate value of resources used

**Full Cost:**
+ Covers all costs
+ Simple to calculate
- May lead to suboptimal decisions
- Fixed costs aren't always relevant

**Negotiated:**
+ Preserves autonomy
+ Flexible
