- Time-consuming
- Depends on negotiating power

sections.title: Range of Acceptable Transfer Prices
sections.content:
**For voluntary internal trade:**

**Minimum (Selling Division):**
• Variable cost + Opportunity cost
• Won't accept less - would lose money

**Maximum (Buying Division):**
• External purchase price
• Won't pay more - would buy outside

**If Range Exists (Min < Max):**
• Trade is mutually beneficial
• Negotiate within range

**If No Range (Min > Max):**
• No mutually beneficial price
• Buying division should buy externally
• Selling division should sell externally

**Company Perspective:**
Encourage trade if company contribution is positive

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: International Transfer Pricing
sections.content:
**Tax authorities watch transfer prices!**

**Issue:**
• Multinational companies can shift profits
• High price to low-tax country
• Reduces overall taxes

**Requirements:**
• "Arm's length" pricing
• Price that unrelated parties would use
• Documentation required

**IRS Methods:**
• Comparable uncontrolled price
• Resale price method
• Cost plus method
• Profit split

**Penalties:**
• Significant fines for non-compliance
• Double taxation possible

**CMA Exam:** Focus on internal management, but know tax implications exist.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Transfer pricing affects division profits and company decisions
Methods: Market, Variable cost, Full cost, Cost-plus, Negotiated, Dual
General rule: TP = Variable Cost + Opportunity Cost
Market price is ideal when competitive market exists
Acceptable range: Selling division's minimum to Buying's maximum
No single method achieves all objectives perfectly
International TP requires arm's length pricing for tax purposes

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A can sell externally for $50 with variable cost of $30 and is at full capacity. Division B wants to buy from A. What is the minimum acceptable transfer price for Division A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Minimum TP = Variable cost + Opportunity cost = $30 + ($50-$30) = $50. Since A is at full capacity, selling internally means losing an external sale at $50. The opportunity cost is the lost contribution margin of $20.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When at full capacity, the opportunity cost is the contribution margin lost from the external sale foregone.

title: Operational Efficiency Metrics
description: Measure and improve operational performance using key efficiency metrics

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Financial metrics alone don't tell the whole story! CMAs need operational metrics to understand what drives financial results. Efficiency metrics connect shop floor activities to financial outcomes.

sections.title: Key Efficiency Metrics
sections.content:
**Capacity Metrics:**
• Theoretical Capacity - Maximum possible
• Practical Capacity - Allowing for maintenance, setup
• Normal Capacity - Average expected use
• Actual Capacity - What was achieved

**Utilization Rate:**
= Actual Output / Capacity
• Higher = better asset use
• But 100% may cause burnout

**Efficiency Rate:**
= Standard Hours / Actual Hours
• > 100% = Better than standard
• < 100% = Worse than standard

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: MCE Analysis
sections.content:
**Manufacturing Cycle Efficiency (MCE):**
= Processing Time / Total Cycle Time

**Example:**
• Processing: 2 hours
• Waiting: 10 hours
• Moving: 1 hour
• Inspection: 0.5 hours
• Total Cycle Time: 13.5 hours

MCE = 2 / 13.5 = 14.8%

**Interpretation:**
• Only 15% of time is value-added!
• 85% is waste (waiting, moving, inspecting)
• World-class target: > 50%
• Most companies: 5-25%

**Improvement focus:** Reduce non-processing time

sections.title: Productivity Metrics
sections.content:
**Labor Productivity:**
= Output / Labor Hours
• Units per labor hour
• Revenue per employee

**Machine Productivity:**
= Output / Machine Hours
• Units per machine hour
• OEE (see below)

**Total Factor Productivity:**
= Output / (Labor + Capital + Materials + Energy)
• Most comprehensive
• Harder to calculate

**Partial vs Total:**
• Partial - one input category
• Total - all inputs combined
• Watch for trade-offs between inputs

sections.title: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)
sections.content:
**OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**

**Availability:**
= Run Time / Planned Production Time
• Losses: Breakdowns, changeovers

**Performance:**
= (Ideal Cycle Time × Output) / Run Time
• Losses: Small stops, slow cycles

**Quality:**
= Good Units / Total Units
• Losses: Defects, rework

**Benchmarks:**
• World-class: 85%+
• Typical: 60%
• Each component should be 95%+

**Example:** 90% × 95% × 99% = 84.6% OEE

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Efficiency vs Effectiveness
sections.content:
**Don't confuse these!**

**Efficiency:**
• Doing things right
• Input/output ratio
• Minimizing waste
• Cost per unit

**Effectiveness:**
• Doing the right things
• Achieving goals
• Customer satisfaction
• Market results

**You can be:**
• Efficient but ineffective (doing wrong things well)
• Effective but inefficient (right things, high cost)

**Goal:** Both efficient AND effective
• Operational excellence + Strategic alignment

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capacity: Theoretical > Practical > Normal > Actual
Utilization = Actual / Capacity
MCE = Processing Time / Total Cycle Time (target >50%)
OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality (target >85%)
Most companies have MCE of 5-25% - huge waste
Productivity = Output / Input for any resource
Balance efficiency (doing things right) with effectiveness (right things)

knowledgeCheck.question: A product has processing time of 3 hours, wait time of 12 hours, move time of 2 hours, and inspection time of 1 hour. What is the Manufacturing Cycle Efficiency?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: MCE = Processing Time / Total Cycle Time = 3 / (3 + 12 + 2 + 1) = 3 / 18 = 16.7%. Only about 1/6 of total time is actually adding value. This suggests major opportunities to reduce waiting and moving time.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Add all times for total cycle time, then divide processing time by that total.

title: Supply Chain Cost Management
description: Analyze and optimize costs across the entire supply chain

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Supply chain can be 50-70% of product cost! CMAs analyze supply chain costs to find savings beyond just purchase price. Understanding total cost of ownership helps make better sourcing decisions and identify improvement opportunities.

sections.title: Supply Chain Cost Categories
sections.content:
**Procurement Costs:**
• Purchase price
• Ordering costs
• Supplier qualification
• Expediting

**Logistics Costs:**
• Transportation (inbound/outbound)
• Warehousing
• Material handling
• Packaging

**Inventory Costs:**
• Carrying costs
• Stockout costs
• Obsolescence
• Shrinkage

**Quality Costs:**
• Inspection
• Returns
• Rework from supplier defects

**Risk Costs:**
• Supply disruption
• Price volatility
• Supplier financial distress

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: TCO Analysis
sections.content:
**"PIQRS" - Total Cost Components:**

**P**urchase price
• Unit cost × quantity
• Volume discounts

**I**nbound logistics
• Freight, customs, handling
• Mode selection

**Q**uality costs
• Inspection, defect rate
• Rework from supplier issues

**R**elationship costs
• Supplier management time
• Communication, visits

**S**ervice/support costs
• Lead time (inventory impact)
• Flexibility/responsiveness
• Technical support

**TCO often shows 'cheap' supplier is expensive!

sections.title: Supplier Cost Management
sections.content:
**Supplier Scorecards:**
• Quality metrics
• Delivery performance
• Cost competitiveness
• Responsiveness

**Negotiation Approaches:**
• Competitive bidding
• Cost breakdown analysis
• Should-cost models
• Long-term contracts

**Collaboration Methods:**
• Early supplier involvement
• Joint cost reduction
• Open book costing
• Value engineering

**Consolidation vs Diversification:**
• Consolidate for volume leverage
• Diversify for risk management
• Balance both factors

sections.title: Supply Chain Analytics
sections.content:
**Key Metrics:**
• Days of Supply
• Inventory Turns
• Perfect Order Rate
• On-Time In-Full (OTIF)
• Total Supply Chain Cost %

**Spend Analysis:**
• Total spend by category
• Spend by supplier
• Maverick spend (off-contract)
• Savings tracking

**CMA Applications:**
• Make-or-buy analysis
• Outsourcing decisions
• Supplier selection
• Network optimization
• Inventory investment

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Lowest Price Isn't Lowest Cost
sections.content:
**Common scenario on CMA exam:**

Supplier A: $10/unit, 3% defect rate
Supplier B: $11/unit, 0.5% defect rate

**Naive answer:** Choose A (lower price)

**Right analysis:** Calculate total cost
• Include rework cost per defect
• Include inspection costs
• Include customer returns
• Include supply disruption risk

**Hidden costs often reverse the decision!**

**Same for:**
• Distant supplier (lower price, higher freight)
• Long lead time (lower price, higher inventory)
• Payment terms (lower price, worse cash flow)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Supply chain: 50-70% of product cost
TCO includes: Price, logistics, quality, relationship, service costs
Low price often ≠ low total cost
Supplier scorecards: Quality, delivery, cost, responsiveness
Collaboration: Early involvement, joint cost reduction
Key metrics: Days of supply, turns, OTIF, perfect order rate
Balance consolidation (leverage) vs diversification (risk)

knowledgeCheck.question: Supplier X offers parts at $8 each with 5% defect rate. Supplier Y offers $9 each with 1% defect rate. Each defect costs $30 to rework. For 1,000 units, which supplier has lower total cost?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Supplier X: ($8 × 1,000) + (50 defects × $30) = $8,000 + $1,500 = $9,500. Supplier Y: ($9 × 1,000) + (10 defects × $30) = $9,000 + $300 = $9,300. Despite higher unit price, Supplier Y has lower total cost due to better quality.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate: (Unit price × quantity) + (Defects × rework cost)

title: Process Improvement and Lean
description: Apply lean principles and continuous improvement methods

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CMAs partner with operations to improve processes and reduce costs! Understanding lean and continuous improvement frameworks helps quantify improvement benefits, track progress metrics, and provide financial support for process excellence initiatives.

sections.title: Lean Management Principles
sections.content:
**Core Lean Philosophy:**
Maximize value, minimize waste

**Five Lean Principles:**
1. **Value:** Define from customer perspective
2. **Value Stream:** Map all steps, identify waste
3. **Flow:** Ensure smooth, uninterrupted flow
4. **Pull:** Produce only what's demanded
5. **Perfection:** Continuously improve

**Origin:**
Toyota Production System (TPS)

**Goal:**
Deliver exactly what customer wants, when they want it, with minimum waste

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Lean Tools
sections.content:
**Key Lean Tools:**

**5S Workplace Organization:**
• Sort - Remove unnecessary items
• Set in order - Organize what remains
• Shine - Clean and inspect
• Standardize - Create consistent procedures
• Sustain - Maintain discipline

**Kanban:**
• Visual signal system
• Pull-based production
• Limits work-in-process

**Value Stream Mapping:**
• Visualize entire process
• Identify value-add vs waste
• Design future state

**Poka-yoke:**
• Error-proofing
• Prevent mistakes from occurring

sections.title: Kaizen - Continuous Improvement
sections.content:
**Kaizen Philosophy:**
• Small, incremental improvements
• Everyone involved
• Daily improvements
• Low-cost ideas

**Kaizen Events:**
• Focused improvement workshops
• 3-5 day intense effort
• Cross-functional team
• Immediate implementation

**Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA):**
• Plan: Identify problem, plan solution
• Do: Implement on small scale
• Check: Measure results
• Act: Standardize if successful, adjust if not

**CMA Role:**
• Quantify savings
• Track improvement metrics
• Cost-benefit analysis

sections.title: Six Sigma Overview
sections.content:
**Goal:**
Reduce variation and defects to 3.4 per million

**DMAIC Methodology:**
• **Define:** Problem and goals
• **Measure:** Current performance
• **Analyze:** Root causes
• **Improve:** Implement solutions
• **Control:** Sustain improvements

**Key Concepts:**
• Sigma level = standard deviations
• Higher sigma = fewer defects
• Data-driven decision making
• Statistical process control

**Lean Six Sigma:**
Combines lean (eliminate waste) with Six Sigma (reduce variation)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Lean Accounting
sections.content:
**Traditional vs Lean Accounting:**

**Traditional Issues in Lean:**
• Standard costing rewards overproduction
• Labor efficiency focuses on keeping busy
• Detailed tracking is non-value-add

**Lean Accounting Features:**
• Value stream costing
• Focus on flow and throughput
• Simplified transactions
• Visual performance boards
• Box scores (operational/capacity/financial)

**Know the conflict:**
Traditional metrics can undermine lean behaviors!
Example: Producing to absorb overhead creates inventory waste

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Lean: Maximize value, minimize waste (Toyota origin)
8 wastes: DOWNTIME - Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, etc.
5S: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain
Kaizen: Continuous incremental improvement (PDCA cycle)
Six Sigma: Reduce variation via DMAIC (3.4 defects per million)
Lean Six Sigma: Combines both approaches
Lean accounting: Value stream based, simplified tracking

knowledgeCheck.question: Which lean concept involves producing items only when downstream processes signal a need, rather than pushing production based on forecasts?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Pull systems produce only in response to actual demand signals (kanban cards), avoiding overproduction waste. This contrasts with push systems that produce based on forecasts. Poka-yoke is error-proofing, 5S is workplace organization, and value stream mapping is process visualization.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which system produces based on actual demand rather than forecast?

title: Total Quality Management
description: Implement quality management principles for operational excellence

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Quality drives cost and customer satisfaction! CMAs measure quality costs, track quality metrics, and quantify the ROI of quality initiatives. Understanding TQM principles helps CMAs partner with operations to improve performance.

sections.title: What is TQM?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Organization-wide effort to embed quality in all processes

**Core Principles:**
• Customer focus
• Total employee involvement
• Process-centered approach
• Integrated system thinking
• Strategic and systematic
• Continual improvement
• Fact-based decisions
• Communication

**Origin:**
Deming, Juran, Crosby - post-WWII Japan

**Goal:**
Meet or exceed customer expectations while reducing costs

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Deming's 14 Points
sections.content:
**Key Deming Principles:**

**1. Create constancy of purpose**
• Long-term improvement focus

**2. Cease dependence on inspection**
• Build quality in, don't inspect it in

**3. End lowest-price contracts**
• Work with fewer, better suppliers

**4. Drive out fear**
• People must feel safe to report problems

**5. Break down barriers**
• Cross-functional teamwork

**6. Use statistical methods**
• Data-driven improvement

**Remember:**
Deming emphasized SYSTEM causes, not blaming workers
Most problems are system failures (85%), not worker failures

sections.title: Quality Tools
sections.content:
**Seven Basic Quality Tools:**

**1. Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone):**
• Identify root causes
• Categories: Man, Machine, Method, Material

**2. Check Sheet:**
• Data collection form
• Tally defect types/frequencies

**3. Control Chart:**
• Monitor process stability
• UCL, LCL, centerline

**4. Histogram:**
• Distribution of data
• Identify patterns

**5. Pareto Chart:**
• 80/20 rule
• Focus on vital few

**6. Scatter Diagram:**
• Correlation between variables

**7. Flowchart:**
• Process mapping

sections.title: Quality Certifications
sections.content:
**ISO 9000 Series:**
• International quality standards
• ISO 9001 - Quality management system
• Customer focus, process approach
• Documentation requirements
• Third-party certification

**Malcolm Baldrige Award:**
• US national quality award
• Criteria categories:
  - Leadership
  - Strategy
  - Customers
  - Measurement and knowledge
  - Workforce
  - Operations
  - Results

**Benefits of Certification:**
• Customer confidence
• Market access
• Systematic improvement

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: TQM vs Traditional
sections.content:
**TQM Philosophy Shifts:**

**Traditional:**
• Inspect quality in
• Quality costs money
• Workers cause problems
• Meet specifications
• Quality vs cost trade-off

**TQM:**
• Build quality in
• Quality is free (Crosby)
• System causes problems
• Exceed expectations
• Quality reduces cost

**Key Insight:**
The cost of PREVENTING defects is less than the cost of FIXING them

Prevention > Appraisal > Internal failure > External failure (worst!)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: TQM: Organization-wide quality culture, not just QC department
Key thinkers: Deming (system), Juran (trilogy), Crosby (zero defects)
Deming: 85% of problems are system failures
Seven tools: Fishbone, check sheet, control chart, histogram, Pareto, scatter, flowchart
ISO 9001: International quality management standard
Baldrige: US quality award with comprehensive criteria
TQM view: Quality reduces cost (quality is free)

knowledgeCheck.question: According to Deming's philosophy, what percentage of quality problems are caused by the system rather than individual workers?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Deming emphasized that approximately 85% of quality problems result from the system (management-created processes, equipment, training) rather than individual workers. This shifts responsibility to management to improve systems rather than blame workers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Deming's philosophy emphasizes system causes over worker causes.

title: Performance Reporting
description: Design and communicate effective performance reports

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Reports drive decisions and behavior! CMAs design reports that inform action, not just inform. Effective performance reporting focuses attention on what matters, enables accountability, and drives improvement.

sections.title: Reporting Principles
sections.content:
**Effective Reports Are:**
• **Relevant:** Tied to user's decisions
• **Timely:** Available when needed
• **Accurate:** Reliable information
• **Complete:** All material information
• **Understandable:** Clear to intended audience
• **Actionable:** Enable decisions

**Report Design Questions:**
• Who is the audience?
• What decisions do they make?
• What information drives those decisions?
• How often do they need it?
• What format works best?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Exception Reporting
sections.content:
**Management by Exception:**

Focus attention on significant deviations

**How It Works:**
1. Set standards/budgets
2. Track actual results
3. Highlight exceptions (significant variances)
4. Investigate and act on exceptions
5. Don't waste time on items 'within tolerance'

**Thresholds:**
• Absolute: > $10,000 variance
• Percentage: > 5% variance
• Combination: Meet either

**Benefits:**
• Focus scarce attention
• Prioritize investigation
• Don't overwhelm with detail

sections.title: Responsibility Reporting
sections.content:
**Controllability Principle:**
Report only items the manager can control

**Responsibility Center Reports:**
• **Cost Center:** Controllable costs only
• **Revenue Center:** Sales performance
• **Profit Center:** Revenue and controllable costs
• **Investment Center:** Plus asset utilization

**Report Characteristics:**
• Segment contribution format
• Controllable vs allocated
• Budget vs actual
• Variance analysis

**Fairness:**
Don't hold managers accountable for uncontrollable items

sections.title: Variance Report Format
sections.content:
**Typical Format:**

| Item | Budget | Actual | Variance | % |
|------|--------|--------|----------|---|
| Revenue | $100K | $95K | ($5K) U | 5% |
| Materials | $40K | $42K | ($2K) U | 5% |
| Labor | $30K | $28K | $2K F | 7% |

**Key Elements:**
• Budget/standard
• Actual
• Variance ($ and %)
• Favorable (F) / Unfavorable (U)
• Prior period comparison (optional)

**Drill-Down:**
Summary to detail capability

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Controllable vs Traceable
sections.content:
**Different Purposes!**

**Controllable:**
• Manager can influence
• Use for performance evaluation
• Example: Direct costs, discretionary spending

**Traceable:**
• Can be traced to segment
• Use for segment profitability
• May not be controllable by segment manager

**Example:**
Building depreciation:
• Traceable to plant manager's segment
• NOT controllable by plant manager
• Don't use for manager evaluation!

**Fair Evaluation:**
Use controllable costs for manager accountability

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Effective reports: Relevant, timely, accurate, actionable
Design for audience and decisions they make
Exception reporting: Focus on significant variances
Management by exception: Set thresholds, investigate outliers
Responsibility reporting: Match to manager's control
Controllable costs for manager evaluation
Traceable costs for segment profitability analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: Which approach focuses management attention on items that deviate significantly from expectations?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Management by exception focuses attention on significant variances from standards or budgets, rather than reviewing all items. This allows managers to use their limited time investigating and addressing the most important deviations.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which approach highlights deviations from expected performance?

title: Materials and Labor Variances
description: Calculate and analyze direct cost variances

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Variance analysis is the HEART of performance management! CMAs calculate variances to identify where actual results differ from plan and WHY. This enables corrective action and continuous improvement. Direct materials and labor variances are heavily tested on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Variance Framework
sections.content:
**Basic Formula:**
Total Variance = Actual - Standard

**Favorable (F):** Actual < Standard (good)
**Unfavorable (U):** Actual > Standard (bad)

**For Direct Costs:**
Total Variance = Price Variance + Quantity Variance

**Price Variance:**
Difference in RATE/PRICE
Actual vs standard price

**Quantity Variance:**
Difference in EFFICIENCY
Actual vs standard usage

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Three-Box Method
sections.content:
**Materials Variances:**

```
      Box 1          Box 2          Box 3
      AQ × AP        AQ × SP        SQ × SP
     (Actual)     (Flex Budget)  (Standard)
         |____________|____________|
             MPV            MQV
```

**Where:**
• AQ = Actual quantity used
• AP = Actual price paid
• SP = Standard price
• SQ = Standard quantity allowed

**Example:**
• AQ = 5,200 lbs
• AP = $4.10
• SP = $4.00
• SQ = 5,000 lbs (1,000 units × 5 lbs/unit)

• MPV = ($4.10 - $4.00) × 5,200 = $520 U
• MQV = (5,200 - 5,000) × $4.00 = $800 U

sections.title: Labor Variance Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Standard: 2 hours @ $15/hour
• Actual production: 500 units
• Actual hours: 1,050
• Actual rate: $15.50/hour

**Standard hours allowed:**
500 units × 2 hours = 1,000 hours

**Labor Rate Variance:**
($15.50 - $15.00) × 1,050 = $525 U

**Labor Efficiency Variance:**
(1,050 - 1,000) × $15.00 = $750 U

**Interpretation:**
• LRV: Paid more per hour than standard
• LEV: Used more hours than should have

sections.title: Variance Interpretation
sections.content:
**Materials Price Variance:**
• **U:** Market price increase, rush orders, quality change
• **F:** Volume discounts, new supplier, lower quality

**Materials Quantity Variance:**
• **U:** Waste, theft, poor quality materials, untrained workers
• **F:** Skilled workers, better equipment, quality materials

**Labor Rate Variance:**
• **U:** Higher skilled workers, overtime premium
• **F:** Lower skilled workers, new hires

**Labor Efficiency Variance:**
• **U:** Untrained workers, poor materials, equipment issues
• **F:** Skilled workers, improved processes

**Interrelationship:** Trade-offs exist (cheap materials → more waste)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Timing of MPV
sections.content:
**When to Calculate MPV?**

**At Purchase:**
• MPV = (AP - SP) × AQ PURCHASED
• More timely
• Isolates purchasing responsibility
• AQ = quantity purchased

**At Usage:**
• MPV = (AP - SP) × AQ USED
• AQ = quantity used in production
• Delays variance recognition

**Common Exam Scenario:**
Purchased 6,000 lbs, used 5,200 lbs
• At purchase: Use 6,000 lbs
• At usage: Use 5,200 lbs

**Read carefully** which quantity to use!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Total variance = Price variance + Quantity variance
MPV = (AP - SP) × AQ (Purchasing responsibility)
MQV = (AQ - SQ) × SP (Production responsibility)
LRV = (AR - SR) × AH (HR responsibility)
LEV = (AH - SH) × SR (Production responsibility)
SQ allowed = Actual output × Standard per unit
Consider trade-offs between price and efficiency

knowledgeCheck.question: Standard: 3 lbs @ $5/lb. Produced 1,000 units. Used 3,200 lbs @ $4.80/lb. What is the materials quantity variance?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: MQV = (AQ - SQ) × SP. SQ allowed = 1,000 units × 3 lbs = 3,000 lbs. MQV = (3,200 - 3,000) × $5.00 = 200 × $5 = $1,000 U. We used 200 more pounds than standard, costed at standard price.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate standard quantity allowed first.

title: Overhead Variance Analysis
description: Analyze variable and fixed overhead variances

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Overhead is often the largest cost category! CMAs must analyze overhead variances to control these significant costs. Understanding the four-variance method (or three-way analysis) is essential for CMA exam success. This is one of the most complex but important variance topics.

sections.title: Overhead Variance Overview
sections.content:
**Variable Overhead:**
• Changes with production activity
• Examples: Indirect materials, utilities
• Two variances: Spending and Efficiency

**Fixed Overhead:**
• Stays constant regardless of volume
• Examples: Rent, depreciation, salaries
• Two variances: Spending and Volume

**Allocation Base:**
• Usually direct labor hours or machine hours
• Connects overhead to products

**Applied Overhead:**
= Standard rate × Standard hours allowed

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Variable Overhead Variances
sections.content:
**VOH Spending Variance:**
Actual VOH
- (Actual Hours × Standard Rate)
= VOH Spending Variance

**What it measures:**
Did we pay more/less per hour of activity?

**VOH Efficiency Variance:**
(Actual Hours - Standard Hours) × Std Rate
= VOH Efficiency Variance

**What it measures:**
Did we use more/fewer hours than should have?

**Note:** VOH efficiency is SAME calculation as labor efficiency!
Both use (AH - SH) × Rate
If labor efficiency is bad, VOH efficiency is bad too!

sections.title: Fixed Overhead Variances
sections.content:
**FOH Budget (Spending) Variance:**
Actual FOH - Budgeted FOH
= FOH Spending Variance

**What it measures:**
Did we spend more/less than budgeted?
Controlled by management decisions

**FOH Volume Variance:**
Budgeted FOH - Applied FOH
= FOH Volume Variance

**Applied FOH = SH allowed × FOH Rate**

**What it measures:**
Capacity utilization
Produced more/less than denominator activity?

**Key insight:**
Volume variance is NOT controllable by management
It's a result of production volume decisions

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Standard: 2 DLH per unit at $5 VOH rate, $3 FOH rate
• Budgeted production: 10,000 units (20,000 hours)
• Budgeted FOH: $60,000
• Actual: 9,500 units, 19,500 hours
• Actual VOH: $100,000
• Actual FOH: $62,000

**SH allowed = 9,500 × 2 = 19,000 hours**

**VOH Spending:**
$100,000 - (19,500 × $5) = $100,000 - $97,500 = $2,500 U

**VOH Efficiency:**
(19,500 - 19,000) × $5 = 500 × $5 = $2,500 U

**FOH Spending:**
$62,000 - $60,000 = $2,000 U

**FOH Volume:**
$60,000 - (19,000 × $3) = $60,000 - $57,000 = $3,000 U

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Volume Variance Interpretation
sections.content:
**FOH Volume Variance:**

**Unfavorable:**
• Produced LESS than budgeted
• Fixed costs spread over fewer units
• Under-applied overhead

**Favorable:**
• Produced MORE than budgeted
• Fixed costs spread over more units
• Over-applied overhead

**Common Confusion:**
• This is NOT a spending variance!
• You didn't spend more or less
• It's about UTILIZATION of capacity

**Controllability:**
• Spending variances = Controllable
• Volume variance = Usually uncontrollable
  (Depends on production decisions, demand)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four variances: VOH spending, VOH efficiency, FOH spending, FOH volume
VOH spending: Price side - cost per hour vs standard
VOH efficiency: Same as labor efficiency - hours control
FOH spending: Actual vs budgeted fixed costs
FOH volume: Capacity utilization (budgeted vs applied)
Volume variance is NOT controllable - it's about utilization
Applied OH = Standard hours allowed × Standard rate

knowledgeCheck.question: Budgeted FOH is $120,000 for 10,000 standard hours. Actual production allowed 9,200 standard hours. What is the FOH volume variance?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: FOH rate = $120,000 / 10,000 = $12/hour. Applied FOH = 9,200 × $12 = $110,400. Volume variance = Budgeted - Applied = $120,000 - $110,400 = $9,600 U. Unfavorable because we produced less than budgeted capacity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate FOH rate first, then applied FOH.

title: Sales Volume and Mix Variances
description: Analyze revenue variances by volume and product mix

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue drives the business! CMAs analyze sales variances to understand WHY revenue differed from budget. Was it price? Was it volume? Was it the product mix? These insights guide sales strategy and forecasting improvements.

sections.title: Sales Variance Overview
sections.content:
**Total Sales Variance:**
= Actual Revenue - Budgeted Revenue

**Breaks Down Into:**
• Sales Price Variance
• Sales Volume Variance

**Volume Further Breaks Into:**
• Sales Mix Variance
• Sales Quantity Variance

**Why Decompose?**
• Different causes
• Different responsibilities
• Different actions needed
• Better performance evaluation

sections.title: Sales Variance Formulas
rows: (Actual Mix - Budget Mix) × Actual Total Qty × Budget CM
(Actual Total - Budget Total) × Budget Mix × Budget CM

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Volume vs Mix vs Quantity
sections.content:
**Sales Volume Variance:**
Total effect of selling different quantities
Combines both effects below

**Sales Mix Variance:**
• Did we sell the RIGHT MIX of products?
• High-margin vs low-margin products
• Holding total volume constant
• Example: More premium, less basic

**Sales Quantity Variance:**
• Did we sell the RIGHT AMOUNT overall?
• Total units vs budget
• At budgeted mix
• Example: Market grew/shrank

**Relationship:**
Volume = Mix + Quantity

**Memory:**
Mix = WHAT we sold (product composition)
Quantity = HOW MUCH total we sold

sections.title: Example Calculation
sections.content:
**Budget:**
• Product A: 1,000 units × $50 CM = $50,000
• Product B: 4,000 units × $20 CM = $80,000
• Total: 5,000 units, $130,000 CM
• Budget mix: A = 20%, B = 80%
• Budget avg CM: $26

**Actual:**
• Product A: 1,500 units × $50 CM = $75,000
• Product B: 4,500 units × $20 CM = $90,000
• Total: 6,000 units, $165,000 CM
• Actual mix: A = 25%, B = 75%

**Sales Volume Variance:**
$165,000 - $130,000 = $35,000 F

**Sales Quantity:**
(6,000 - 5,000) × $26 = $26,000 F

**Sales Mix:**
$35,000 - $26,000 = $9,000 F
(Shifted toward higher-margin Product A)

sections.title: Contribution Margin Approach
sections.content:
**Why Use CM for Sales Variances?**
• CM reflects true profit impact
• Revenue alone doesn't show profit effect
• Different products have different margins

**CM-Based Analysis:**
• More informative for management
• Shows profit impact directly
• Highlights mix effects

**Alternative: Revenue Approach:**
• Uses selling price instead of CM
• Shows revenue impact
• Doesn't show profit impact

**CMA Exam:** Usually uses CM approach

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Market Size and Share
sections.content:
**Advanced Decomposition:**

**Sales Quantity can split into:**
• Market Size Variance
• Market Share Variance

**Market Size:**
• Did the total market grow/shrink?
• Uncontrollable by company

**Market Share:**
• Did we gain/lose share?
• Controllable, performance-related

**Example:**
• Budget: 10% share of 50,000 market = 5,000
• Actual: 12% share of 60,000 market = 7,200
• Market size effect: (60K - 50K) × 10% = 1,000 units
• Market share effect: (12% - 10%) × 60K = 1,200 units

**CMA Exam:** Know conceptually, detailed math less common

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sales price variance: Different price × actual volume
Sales volume variance: Different volume × budgeted CM
Volume = Mix variance + Quantity variance
Mix: Selling different product proportions
Quantity: Selling different total units
Use contribution margin for profit impact
Favorable mix = More high-margin products sold

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's actual sales shifted toward products with higher contribution margins while total sales volume remained at budget. This would result in:
options: No variance since total volume is at budget
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A shift toward higher-margin products creates a favorable sales mix variance. Since total volume is at budget, the quantity variance is zero. The favorable mix shows the company optimized its product composition.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Mix variance measures the effect of selling different product proportions.

title: Flexible Budgets and Performance Reports
description: Create budgets that adjust for activity levels

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Static budgets become meaningless when activity changes! Flexible budgets adjust expectations to actual volume, giving fair performance evaluations. This is how real-world performance analysis works.

sections.title: Static vs Flexible Budgets
sections.content:
**Static Budget:**
• Prepared for ONE activity level
• Does not change with actual volume
• Useful for fixed costs
• Unfair for variable costs

**Flexible Budget:**
• Adjusts to ACTUAL activity level
• 'What should we have spent at this volume?'
• Fair comparison for variable costs
• Separates volume from efficiency

**Key Insight:**
If actual volume differs from budget, static comparison is apples to oranges

sections.title: Flexible Budget Formula
sections.content:
**Flexible Budget = Fixed Costs + (Variable Cost per Unit × Actual Units)**

**Example:**
Static budget: 10,000 units
Actual production: 12,000 units

Variable cost: $5 per unit
Fixed costs: $50,000

**Static Budget:**
$5 × 10,000 + $50,000 = $100,000

**Flexible Budget:**
$5 × 12,000 + $50,000 = $110,000

**Comparison:**
Compare actual costs to $110,000
Not to original $100,000 static budget

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Variance Breakdown
sections.content:
**Total Variance = Volume Variance + Flexible Budget Variance**

**Volume Variance:**
(Actual Units - Budgeted Units) × Budgeted CM per unit
• Did we sell more or less than planned?
• Usually marketing/sales responsibility

**Flexible Budget Variance:**
Actual - Flexible Budget
• At actual volume, did we spend as expected?
• Isolates efficiency/price issues
• Operations responsibility

**Key Insight:**
• Volume variance: Activity level impact
• Flex variance: Efficiency/spending impact
• Together: Total static variance

sections.title: Performance Report Example
sections.content:
**Example Performance Report:**

Actual units: 12,000
Budgeted units: 10,000

| Item | Actual | Flexible Budget | Flex Variance | Static Budget | Volume Variance |
|------|--------|-----------------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Units | 12,000 | 12,000 | - | 10,000 | 2,000 F |
| Revenue | $228,000 | $240,000 | $12,000 U | $200,000 | $40,000 F |
| Variable | $145,000 | $144,000 | $1,000 U | $120,000 | $24,000 U |
| CM | $83,000 | $96,000 | $13,000 U | $80,000 | $16,000 F |

**Analysis:**
• Volume helped (+2,000 units)
• But price was lower ($12,000 U)
• And variable costs higher ($1,000 U)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Variance Signs
sections.content:
**Favorable vs Unfavorable:**

**For Revenue:**
• Actual > Budget = Favorable
• Actual < Budget = Unfavorable

**For Costs:**
• Actual < Budget = Favorable (spent less!)
• Actual > Budget = Unfavorable (overspent!)

**Common Exam Mistake:**
Confusing direction of variance for costs

**Memory:**
'Good for the company = Favorable'
• More revenue = F
• Less cost = F

**Net Income Impact:**
Favorable increases income
Unfavorable decreases income

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Static budget: One activity level, does not adjust
Flexible budget: Adjusts to actual activity level
Flexible = Fixed + (Variable rate × Actual units)
Volume variance: Budget vs Flex (activity impact)
Flex variance: Flex vs Actual (efficiency impact)
Revenue favorable: Actual > Budget
Cost favorable: Actual < Budget

knowledgeCheck.question: A static budget was prepared for 8,000 units with variable costs of $40,000. Actual production was 10,000 units with actual variable costs of $52,000. What is the flexible budget variance for variable costs?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Variable cost per unit = $40,000 / 8,000 = $5. Flexible budget at 10,000 units = $5 × 10,000 = $50,000. Flexible budget variance = Actual - Flexible = $52,000 - $50,000 = $2,000 Unfavorable (spent more than expected at actual volume).
knowledgeCheck.hint: First calculate the flexible budget at actual volume, then compare to actual costs.
